title: Pluggable prompt input renderer — the missing half of produce mode
description:
Produce mode has three output strategies (structured / json_mode / text) but only one input strategy: JSON dump. This asymmetry is visible in every production pipeline the moment a produce node receives a Pydantic model with prose or nested structure — the LLM sees escaped \n-laden JSON and the consumer pays the token cost.

**SCOPE:** This issue is about INPUT rendering only. Output schema emission is handled by output_strategy — see sibling issue for TypeScript-style output schema improvement to the json_mode path.

## The problem

When decompose(read_node: ReadContext) runs, its input is serialized via model_dump_json(indent=2) and dropped into the prompt. For ReadContext with a 5KB markdown file in raw: str, the LLM sees 8KB of escaped JSON with backslash-n everywhere. Every piarch pipeline will have this issue — ~60-80 distinct models containing prose across 15 artifact types.

No existing framework solves this cleanly:
- DSPy: delimited field headers + per-type dispatch (close, but still JSON-dumps nested models)
- BAML: user-directed Jinja templating (punts rendering to the author)
- Pydantic AI: format_as_xml helper (good but isolated, not integrated with templating)
- LangChain: f-string interpolation with str() coercion (worst)

## Proposed design — three-layer approach

### Layer 1: Pluggable Renderer protocol

class Renderer(Protocol):
    def render(self, model: BaseModel) -> dict[str, str]: ...

Three built-ins:
- XmlRenderer (default) — Pydantic AI format_as_xml style with include_field_info='once'
- DelimitedRenderer — DSPy-style [[ ## field ## ]] with per-type dispatch
- JsonRenderer — current behavior, opt-in backward compat

Output is dict[str, str] — pre-rendered string values per field, ready to drop into a Mustache/Jinja template.

### Layer 2: Per-node/per-model override

@node(renderer=XmlRenderer(include_field_info='once'))
def decompose(read_node: ReadContext) -> ClaimGroupingResult: ...

Or at model level:
class ReadContext(BaseModel):
    model_config = {'neograph_renderer': 'xml'}
    ...

Or full custom via method:
class ReadContext(BaseModel):
    def render_for_prompt(self) -> str: ...

Dispatch order: method > model config > node override > graph default.

### Layer 3: Inspector

graph.render_prompt(node_name, input) -> str

Returns the exact string the LLM would see, without making an LLM call. Matches BAML's VS Code playground and DSPy's inspect_history(). Critical for prompt engineering iteration.

## What it does NOT change

- Templating stays separate (Mustache/Jinja)
- Registry stays separate (Langfuse/LangSmith Hub/Braintrust)
- Output strategies (structured/json_mode/text) unchanged
- The prompt_compiler hook in configure_llm stays as the final override

## IMPLEMENTATION NOTE: port, do not import

PORT the algorithm from these sources, do NOT take them as dependencies:

1. DSPy's format_field_value() from dspy/adapters/utils.py (~40 lines) — per-type dispatch for str/int/list/dict/BaseModel. Apache-2.0, port with attribution.

2. Pydantic AI's format_as_xml() from pydantic_ai.format — XML rendering with include_field_info='once'. MIT, port or import (small scope, clean API).

3. Field description extraction from Pydantic's native Field(description='...') metadata — no external dep needed.

Rationale against importing DSPy as a library:
- DSPy owns the LM call via dspy.LM, conflicts with LangChain/langfuse
- DSPy adapters work on Signatures, not Pydantic models — requires translation
- DSPy Predict drives the whole call; using adapter alone means calling semi-private functions
- ~50MB of transitive deps (datasets, optuna, litellm, etc.) for 300 lines of rendering
- DSPy's 'programming not prompting' philosophy conflicts with @node's 'functions ARE the prompt structure'

Porting takes ~1 day, understanding DSPy enough to use it right takes a week, and porting gives us full control over rendering for eval determinism.

## Concrete piarch examples

ReadContext — large raw markdown field, small scalar fields
DecompositionResult — list of RawClaim with prose statements
ClusterMatchResult — dict of dispositions with evidence text
StakeholderInterests — nested stakeholders with interests list
UCOverview — multi-section prose document
BRDocument — structured fields all containing prose
BDDFeature — Gherkin scenarios with steps

All 15 piarch pipelines have models like these. Current JSON dump is visibly wrong for every one.

## References

- DSPy ChatAdapter format_field_value — dspy/adapters/chat_adapter.py + dspy/adapters/utils.py
- Pydantic AI format_as_xml — ai.pydantic.dev/api/format_as_xml/
- Anthropic prompting guide — XML-for-inputs rationale
- Research report: see piarch constructs research session on 2026-04-05

## Success criteria

1. ReadContext renders as markdown block with sections list as bullets, not JSON
2. DecompositionResult renders as numbered claims with prose, not JSON array
3. Token count per render ≥30% lower than current JSON dump for prose-heavy models
4. Users can inspect rendered prompts without calling LLM via graph.render_prompt()
5. Backward compat via JsonRenderer opt-in
6. Zero changes to Langfuse/prompt registry integration
7. Zero new heavy dependencies (no dspy-ai, no baml, no litellm)
design:
## Architecture (from plan session 2026-04-06)

### Dispatch hierarchy (4 levels + backward compat)

| Level | Where set | How |
|-------|-----------|-----|
| 1. Model method | `def render_for_prompt(self) -> str` on BaseModel | Most specific. Checked per-value inside render_input() before any renderer runs. |
| 2. Node | `Node('x', renderer=XmlRenderer())` or `@node(renderer=...)` | Per-node override. |
| 3. Construct | `Construct('p', renderer=DelimitedRenderer(), nodes=[...])` | Pipeline default. Propagated to child nodes via __init__ (same pattern as llm_config). |
| 4. Global | `configure_llm(..., renderer=XmlRenderer())` | Applies to all pipelines. Checked at factory render time. |
| 5. None | No renderer configured | Raw Pydantic model to prompt_compiler. Identical to today. |

Resolution: model method > node.renderer (may be from Construct propagation) > global > None.

### New file: src/neograph/renderers.py

```python
@runtime_checkable
class Renderer(Protocol):
    def render(self, value: Any) -> str: ...

class XmlRenderer:  # Pydantic AI format_as_xml style
    def __init__(self, *, include_field_info: Literal['once', 'always', 'never'] = 'once'): ...

class DelimitedRenderer:  # DSPy-style [[ ## field ## ]] headers
class JsonRenderer:       # Explicit opt-in for model_dump_json

def render_input(input_data: Any, *, renderer: Renderer | None) -> Any:
    # For dict-form (fan-in): render per-value. Checks render_for_prompt() first.
```

### Integration points

**Node + Construct fields:**
- node.py: add `renderer: Any = None`
- construct.py: add `renderer: Any = None` + propagation in __init__ (same llm_config pattern)
- decorators.py: add `renderer=` kwarg to @node, pass through to Node()

**Factory (between _extract_input and invoke_*):**
```python
input_data = _extract_input(state, node)
effective_renderer = node.renderer or _get_global_renderer()
rendered = render_input(input_data, renderer=effective_renderer)
result = invoke_structured(..., input_data=rendered, ...)
```
Scripted nodes NOT rendered (Python code, not LLM prompts).

**configure_llm:**
```python
def configure_llm(llm_factory, prompt_compiler, *, renderer=None): ...
```

**Inspector:**
```python
def render_prompt(node: Node, input_data: Any, *, config=None) -> str:
    # Returns exact prompt without LLM call. Public export.
```

### Files to modify
- src/neograph/renderers.py (NEW)
- src/neograph/node.py (1 field)
- src/neograph/construct.py (field + propagation)
- src/neograph/decorators.py (kwarg passthrough)
- src/neograph/factory.py (render_input in produce + tool fns)
- src/neograph/_llm.py (configure_llm renderer kwarg, render_prompt)
- src/neograph/__init__.py (exports)

### Tests (~29 tests in 6 classes)
TestRendererDispatch (10): all 5 levels, fan-in dict per-value, model method wins, None passthrough
TestRendererThreeSurfaces (4): @node / declarative / programmatic / ForwardConstruct
TestXmlRenderer (7): flat, nested, lists, include_field_info modes, prose no-escaping
TestDelimitedRenderer (3): flat, nested, lists
TestJsonRenderer (2): model_dump_json, custom indent
TestRenderPromptInspector (3): basic, with renderer, json_mode includes schema

### Depends on
- neograph-7as (describe_type) — renderers reuse it for include_field_info

### Core invariant
Rendering is additive. No renderer configured = raw passthrough = identical to today. The consumer's prompt_compiler contract is unchanged; it just receives pre-rendered strings instead of raw Pydantic models when a renderer is active.

title: prompt_compiler should receive output_model and llm_config for json_mode prompts
description: When output_strategy is json_mode, the prompt compiler needs the output Pydantic model's JSON schema to inject it into the prompt (e.g. 'Return a JSON object matching this schema: ...'). Currently prompt_compiler only receives (template, data, node_name, config). It should also receive output_model and llm_config so consumers can build provider-specific prompts. Without this, json_mode produces FILE blocks instead of JSON because the prompt doesn't ask for JSON.

title: lint() helper: validate DI bindings with sample config
description: Add a lint(graph, config={...}) function that validates all FromInput/FromConfig bindings resolve to non-None. Plus add required=True flag support on FromInput/FromConfig markers to fail fast at runtime instead of silently passing None. Addresses neograph-iio2 (FromInput after fan-out boundary).
notes: ## Research context: Dagster has 'dagster definitions validate' CLI for CI/CD. Consider a 'neograph validate' command that loads a module, builds all constructs, and reports validation errors without executing. The lint() helper could power this.

title: @node: Oracle ensemble kwargs (ensemble_n=, merge_fn=, merge_prompt=)
description:
Part of epic neograph-4mx (@node production readiness). Surfaced by the Path A Dogfood epic.

## Problem

The Oracle modifier (`src/neograph/modifiers.py:127-156`) provides N-way ensemble + judge-merge via `node | Oracle(n=3, merge_prompt='rw/merge')` or `node | Oracle(n=3, merge_fn='combine')`. There is no way to attach an Oracle modifier to a @node-decorated function. Users writing LLM pipelines with ensemble-judge patterns cannot use @node.

Repro:
    # Desired
    @node(mode='produce', output=Claims, prompt='rw/decompose', model='reason',
          ensemble_n=3, merge_prompt='rw/decompose-merge')
    def decompose(topic: RawText) -> Claims: ...

    # Today
    decompose = Node(name='decompose', mode='produce', output=Claims,
                    prompt='rw/decompose', model='reason') | Oracle(n=3, merge_prompt='rw/decompose-merge')
    # Can't combine with @node at all — the Oracle modifier would have to be
    # applied after decoration, and neograph-jyw shows that modifier composition
    # on decorated nodes is broken (sidecar doesn't carry).

## Proposed API

Add three kwargs to `@node`:
- `ensemble_n: int | None = None` — number of parallel generators
- `merge_fn: str | None = None` — registered scripted function name (alternative to merge_prompt)
- `merge_prompt: str | None = None` — LLM judge prompt (alternative to merge_fn)

Semantics:
- If any of the three is set, attach `Oracle(n=ensemble_n, merge_fn=merge_fn, merge_prompt=merge_prompt)` to the Node at decoration time (not at construct_from_module time — the modifier is node-local).
- Validation at decoration time:
  - `ensemble_n` set but neither merge_fn nor merge_prompt → raise ConstructError
  - Both merge_fn and merge_prompt set → raise ConstructError (matches existing Oracle.model_post_init)
  - `ensemble_n` < 2 → raise (ensemble of 1 is pointless)
  - merge_fn or merge_prompt set without ensemble_n → default ensemble_n to 3
- The resulting Node carries the Oracle modifier in its `modifiers` list, same as the declarative path.

## Implementation sketch

In `src/neograph/decorators.py`:

    from neograph.modifiers import Each, Oracle

    def node(
        fn=None,
        *,
        mode='produce',  # or after q54 lands: None (inferred)
        ...,
        ensemble_n: int | None = None,
        merge_fn: str | None = None,
        merge_prompt: str | None = None,
    ):
        ...
        def decorator(f):
            ...
            n = Node(...)
            _register_sidecar(n, f, param_names)

            # Attach Oracle modifier if any ensemble kwarg is set.
            if ensemble_n is not None or merge_fn is not None or merge_prompt is not None:
                if merge_fn is None and merge_prompt is None:
                    raise ConstructError(
                        f\"@node '{node_name}' sets ensemble_n={ensemble_n} but \"
                        f\"neither merge_fn nor merge_prompt. One is required.\"
                    )
                if merge_fn is not None and merge_prompt is not None:
                    raise ConstructError(
                        f\"@node '{node_name}' sets both merge_fn and merge_prompt. \"
                        f\"Choose exactly one.\"
                    )
                n_copies = ensemble_n if ensemble_n is not None else 3
                if n_copies < 2:
                    raise ConstructError(
                        f\"@node '{node_name}' ensemble_n must be >= 2, got {n_copies}.\"
                    )
                # Use `|` to append the modifier (returns a new Node via model_copy).
                # Must re-register the sidecar on the new instance — see neograph-jyw
                # (sidecar carryover on modifier composition). This subtask assumes
                # neograph-jyw has landed.
                n = n | Oracle(n=n_copies, merge_fn=merge_fn, merge_prompt=merge_prompt)
                _register_sidecar(n, f, param_names)

            return n

## Acceptance criteria

- New tests in `TestNodeDecorator`:
  - `test_node_decorator_oracle_with_merge_fn`: `@node(mode='produce', ensemble_n=3, merge_fn='combine')` end-to-end with a FakeLLM and registered merge function; result matches N-way ensemble semantics.
  - `test_node_decorator_oracle_with_merge_prompt`: same with `merge_prompt='judge/prompt'`.
  - `test_node_decorator_oracle_default_n`: `@node(merge_fn='combine')` without `ensemble_n` defaults to `n=3`.
  - `test_node_decorator_oracle_no_merge_raises`: `ensemble_n=3` without merge_fn or merge_prompt raises at decoration time.
  - `test_node_decorator_oracle_both_merge_raises`: both merge_fn and merge_prompt raises.
  - `test_node_decorator_oracle_n_too_small_raises`: `ensemble_n=1` raises.
- Oracle modifier is attached to the Node's `modifiers` list — verifiable via `node.get_modifier(Oracle)`.
- Integration tests from TestOracle still pass unchanged (this adds an API surface, doesn't change semantics).
- Update `@node` docstring with an Oracle usage example.
- Full suite stays green.

## Dependencies

- Blocks on **neograph-jyw** (Each interop via sidecar carryover) — the same sidecar-on-modifier-copy mechanism is reused here for Oracle. Do not duplicate the fix.
- Blocks on **neograph-q54** (mode inference) — for the docstring example. Soft dep, not hard.

## Files to read

- `src/neograph/modifiers.py:127-156` — Oracle class, model_post_init validation
- `src/neograph/decorators.py` — current `@node` decorator
- `tests/test_e2e_piarch_ready.py::TestOracle` — existing Oracle test patterns
- `tests/fakes.py::StructuredFake` — LLM fake for Oracle tests

## Non-goals

- Do NOT edit `src/neograph/modifiers.py` — Oracle class stays as-is.
- Do NOT support Oracle on non-produce modes (ensemble over tool-calling is a different feature).
close_reason: Three modifier-kwargs subtasks on neograph-4mx. nqe: Oracle ensemble_n/merge_fn/merge_prompt kwargs, 6 tests. t5z: mode='raw' support replacing @raw_node, 4 tests. 6y3: Operator interrupt_when kwarg (string + callable forms with auto-registration), 6 tests. 143/143 passing (+16 new).

title: H2: Dunder field traversal in condition evaluator — info disclosure
description: conditions.py _resolve_field walks any dotted path including __class__, __dict__, __module__ via getattr. Condition 'x.__class__.__module__ == "os"' successfully reads internal attributes. Fix: reject path segments starting with underscore in _resolve_field.
close_reason: Fixed in 3eb7fdd with comprehensive regression tests

title: RetryPolicy support for LLM nodes
description:
NeoGraph-compiled graphs have no retry on LLM failures. The classic LangGraph pipeline uses RetryPolicy(max_attempts=3) on all LLM-calling nodes, which handles:
- Malformed JSON from json_mode (R1 occasionally produces broken JSON)
- Pydantic validation errors (LLM output doesn't match schema)
- Transient API errors (429s, timeouts)

Without retry, a single malformed JSON response crashes the entire pipeline. With retry, the node silently retries and usually succeeds on the second attempt.

Options:
1. compile() accepts a retry_policy= that applies to all LLM nodes
2. @node(retry=3) per-node declaration
3. construct_from_functions(..., retry_policy=...) at construct level

The rate limiter already handles 429 retries at the HTTP level. This is about node-level retry for parse/validation failures.

title: Oracle: generators use node.outputs type for LLM schema, but merge_fn changes the type
description:
When a node has Oracle(merge_fn=...) and the merge_fn returns a DIFFERENT type than what each generator should produce, the framework uses node.outputs as the LLM schema for each generator. This is wrong — the generator should produce its own type, and the merge transforms it.

## Concrete case

```python
@node(outputs=ClaimGroupingResult, model='reason', prompt='rw/decompose',
      ensemble_n=3, merge_fn='group_claims')
def decompose(read_node: ReadContext) -> ClaimGroupingResult: ...

@merge_fn
def group_claims(variants: list[DecompositionResult], ...) -> ClaimGroupingResult:
    # Takes 3x DecompositionResult, groups equivalent claims, returns ClaimGroupingResult
    ...
```

What happens:
- outputs=ClaimGroupingResult, so each generator's LLM call uses ClaimGroupingResult.model_json_schema() as the json_mode schema
- The LLM is asked to produce ClaimGroupingResult (which has groups: list[ClaimGroup] with gen_id cross-references) — impossible for a single generator to produce
- The LLM produces something that doesn't parse → generators return None/empty → merge gets empty variants → pipeline produces nothing

What should happen:
- Each generator should produce DecompositionResult (inferred from merge_fn's first parameter type: list[DecompositionResult] → element type is DecompositionResult)
- The framework should use DecompositionResult.model_json_schema() for each generator's LLM call
- The merge_fn receives list[DecompositionResult], returns ClaimGroupingResult
- node.outputs should reflect the POST-MERGE type (ClaimGroupingResult) for downstream consumers

## The type flow

```
Generator LLM call → DecompositionResult (per-generator type, from merge_fn signature)
merge_fn(list[DecompositionResult]) → ClaimGroupingResult (post-merge type, = node.outputs)
downstream node consumes → ClaimGroupingResult (from node.outputs)
```

## How to infer the generator type

The merge_fn's first parameter is typed as list[DecompositionResult]. The element type IS the generator output type. The framework can inspect this at compile time:

```python
import inspect, typing
sig = inspect.signature(merge_fn)
first_param = list(sig.parameters.values())[0]
# first_param.annotation is list[DecompositionResult]
element_type = typing.get_args(first_param.annotation)[0]  # DecompositionResult
```

## Impact

This blocks any Oracle where the merge transforms the type — the most common real-world pattern. The workaround (making generators and merge return the same type) forces unnatural API design.
notes:
## Reproduction
- Test: test_oracle_generator_uses_merge_input_type_not_node_outputs
- Status: FAILING (confirmed bug)
- Log shows 'output=PostMerge' for generator calls — should be PerVariant
- Pydantic validation error: 'Input should be PostMerge' when PerVariant is written to collector
- Root cause: _resolve_primary_output(node) returns node.outputs which is the POST-MERGE type
- Fix locations:
  1. factory.py: _resolve_primary_output or the produce wrapper needs to use the generator type, not node.outputs, when Oracle is active
  2. state.py: compile_state_model builds the collector as list[node.outputs] — should be list[generator_type]
  3. compiler.py: _add_oracle_nodes could store the inferred generator type on the node or pass it to the factory
  4. Inference: inspect merge_fn signature, get_args(first_param.annotation)[0] = element type
## Trace Similar
- The same issue applies to gather/execute modes (not just produce) — _make_tool_fn also uses _resolve_primary_output(node)
- The state model collector type in state.py:_add_output_field (line 169) is Annotated[list[output_type], _collect_oracle_results] where output_type = node.outputs — also wrong for type-transforming merge_fn

## Architect Review
- [MEDIUM] Where to store the generator type? Options:
  A. Node field (node.oracle_generator_type) — requires node.py change (IR layer)
  B. Infer at compile time from merge_fn signature — requires compiler to pass it down
  C. Infer at factory time from merge_fn registry lookup — runtime overhead each call
  PREFER B: infer once at compile time, store result for factory use. The compiler already resolves the merge_fn. Use a new field on Node: oracle_gen_type.
- [LOW] State model collector needs the generator type too (list[gen_type] not list[output_type])
- [LOW] The @node decorator with ensemble_n= should also infer and store the gen type

Overall: ALL_LOW (the MEDIUM is a placement choice, B is clearly best)

title: Dev-mode linter: runtime warnings for ambiguous patterns

title: Add StructuredFakeWithRaw for include_raw=True testing

StructuredFake ignores include_raw kwarg. _call_structured passes include_raw=True by default and expects {parsed: model, raw: AIMessage(usage_metadata=...)} dict back. The fallback code path + usage metadata extraction are untestable with current fakes.

Add StructuredFakeWithRaw variant to fakes.py that returns the raw dict format. Enables testing the default code path through _call_structured.

P1 from test audit.

title: Mini-project: Incident Response Triage
description:
## Structure
```
examples/incident-triage/
  README.md
  pipeline.yaml
  project.yaml
  main.py                # entry point: takes an alert JSON
  tools/
    log_search.py        # search application logs
    metrics_query.py     # query metrics/monitoring
    deployment_history.py # recent deployments
  prompts/
    triage.md
    correlate.md
    rca_draft.md
  schemas.py             # Alert, Evidence, Hypothesis, RCA
  sample_alert.json
  .env.example
```

## Pipeline
1. parse_alert (scripted) — extract alert metadata
2. gather_evidence (Each over data sources [logs, metrics, deploys]):
   - search (agent, tools=[log_search/metrics_query/deployment_history])
3. correlate (think, Loop: hypothesize→test→refine)
4. draft_rca (think, Oracle models=[reason, fast])
5. format_report (scripted) — markdown RCA report

## Features demonstrated
- Each fan-out over data sources
- Loop for hypothesis refinement
- Oracle multi-model RCA drafting
- Real tool calls (simulated log/metrics APIs)
- Time-critical pipeline (observability matters)

title: Tool results in ToolMessage: use describe_type schema + renderer, not model_dump_json
description:
When a tool returns a Pydantic model, the ToolMessage content (what the agent LLM sees during the ReAct loop) should use describe_type for the schema header + the configured renderer for the instance — same as every other prompt input. Currently it uses model_dump_json which is raw JSON values with no field descriptions.

## File: src/neograph/_llm.py, lines ~367-380

### Current (just shipped in neograph-iura):
if isinstance(result, _BM):
    rendered = result.model_dump_json(indent=2)
...
messages.append(ToolMessage(content=rendered, ...))

### Should be:
if isinstance(result, _BM):
    from neograph.describe_type import describe_type
    schema_header = describe_type(type(result))
    # Use the node's renderer (or global) to render the instance
    instance_text = _render_tool_result(result, renderer)
    rendered = f'{schema_header}\n\n{instance_text}'
...
messages.append(ToolMessage(content=rendered, ...))

## What the LLM sees now (model_dump_json):
{
  "source_file": "auth.py",
  "line": 42,
  "snippet": "def authenticate(user, password): ...",
  "relevance": 0.95
}

## What it should see (describe_type + renderer):
Tool result schema:
{
  source_file: string  // Path to the source file
  line: int  // Line number
  snippet: string  // Code snippet at that line
  relevance: float  // Relevance score 0-1
}

<source_file>auth.py</source_file>
<line>42</line>
<snippet>def authenticate(user, password): ...</snippet>
<relevance>0.95</relevance>

## Why this matters
- describe_type includes Field(description=...) annotations — the LLM knows what each field MEANS
- The renderer (XML/delimited/JSON) is consistent with how all other inputs are rendered
- model_dump_json gives values without context — the LLM has to guess field semantics

## Implementation detail
invoke_with_tools needs access to the node's renderer. Currently it doesn't receive it. Two options:
A. Pass renderer as a parameter to invoke_with_tools
B. Use the global renderer via _get_global_renderer() inside invoke_with_tools

Option A is cleaner — the factory already has the node, passes renderer down.

## Scope
- Only the ToolMessage content changes (what the agent LLM sees)
- ToolInteraction.result gets the same rendered text (backward compat: still a string)
- ToolInteraction.typed_result stays the original model (unchanged)
- Downstream nodes receive typed_result — how THEY render it is their business

## Acceptance criteria
- ToolMessage.content includes describe_type schema header for Pydantic tool results
- ToolMessage.content uses the configured renderer for the instance
- Non-Pydantic tool returns (str, dict) unchanged — str() as before
- All existing tests pass
- New e2e test: verify ToolMessage content has schema header + rendered instance

title: Docs: Node.map() lambda fan-out method
description: Node.map(source, key=) provides refactor-safe lambda-based fan-out. Barely documented — mentioned in passing only. Needs dedicated example comparing string-form vs lambda-form.

title: @node context= for verbatim state injection into prompts
description:
Nodes that consume pre-formatted context (graph catalog, domain briefing) need a way to declare state fields that get injected verbatim into the prompt — not BAML-rendered, not part of the typed input model.

Use case: piarch's explore node needs the graph catalog (incident-encoded, BFS-ordered) alongside its typed VerifyClaim input. The catalog is pre-computed by an upstream node and stored in state. It must not be re-rendered — the format is crafted for LLM consumption.

Proposed API:
  @node(
      mode='agent',
      prompt='match-verify/explore',
      context=['build_catalog'],  # state fields injected verbatim
      tools=[search, read],
  )
  def explore(claim: VerifyClaim) -> ExplorationResult: ...

Template uses {context.build_catalog} placeholders. Framework looks up state fields by name, passes strings through as-is. Typed input still BAML-rendered via {input}.

Three rendering strategies unified:
- Typed input (VerifyClaim) → BAML (generic)
- Tool results (SearchResult) → renderer dispatch (custom or BAML)
- Context (catalog, briefing) → verbatim (this feature)
close_reason: context= field on Node + @node, factory extraction, prompt compiler injection. 416 tests.

title: Optional[X] crashes _types_compatible with TypeError
description:
outputs=Optional[Claims] causes TypeError: issubclass() arg 1 must be a class. typing.Union is not a class, but the code calls issubclass(typing.Union, target) at _construct_validation.py line 474.

PEP 604 (X | None via types.UnionType) works fine. Only typing.Optional[X] / typing.Union[X, Y] crashes.

Fix: Add Union/Optional unwrapping at the top of _types_compatible, BEFORE the producer_origin block:
- Producer is Union: strip None, check all non-None members are compatible with target
- Target is Union: strip None, check producer is compatible with any non-None member

This also fixes the Optional[X] consumer false-positive rejection (same root cause, target-side).

Fixtures: tests/check_fixtures/known_gaps/type_optional_producer_crash.py, type_optional_consumer.py

title: Tighten Construct.input/output annotation from Any to type[BaseModel] | None
description:
MED-11 from code review 040426_2207 (PP-11).

src/neograph/construct.py:69-70 declares:
  input: Any = None   # type[BaseModel] | None
  output: Any = None  # type[BaseModel] | None

The comment states the real type. Pydantic v2 with arbitrary_types_allowed=True accepts type[BaseModel] | None directly (verified: pydantic accepts a BaseModel subclass as a type[BaseModel] value). Leave Node.input as Any because of its dict[str, type] case — that's a legitimate union.

Fix construct.py only:
  input: type[BaseModel] | None = None
  output: type[BaseModel] | None = None

Gives better IDE autocomplete and a more honest field contract.

Source: .claude/code-review/040426_2207/synthesis.md MED-11
close_reason: Wave 3 refactors. MED-02 extracted split_each_path helper in modifiers.py; both construct.py static walker and compiler.py runtime walker consume it. MED-06 adopted msg=...; raise idiom for 4 ConstructError sites. MED-09 rewrote _source_location using sys._getframe (dropped inspect import). MED-10 hardened _resolve_field_annotation to return _MISSING for unresolved ForwardRef/str leaks. MED-11 tightened Construct.input/output to type[BaseModel] | None. MED-07 added explanatory comment above _format_no_producer_error documenting the intentional multi-line divergence. 102/102 passing; examples 04/10 continue to run (ForwardRef resolution intact).

title: Oracle merge_fn not firing after generators complete in construct_from_functions pipeline
description:
In piarch's RW pipeline, decompose uses Oracle(n=3, merge_fn='group_claims'). Three generators run and complete (confirmed in logs — 3x node_start/node_complete for decompose). But the merge barrier node (merge_decompose) never fires. The pipeline skips directly from the last decompose completion to flatten-groups, which receives empty/null input and the pipeline ends with no claims.

Log evidence (logs/16c91187.jsonl):
- Events 16-24: 3x decompose start + complete with LLM calls (47s, 76s, 100s)
- Event 25: flatten-groups starts IMMEDIATELY (no merge event in between)
- Event 26: flatten-groups completes in 0.0s (empty result)
- Event 27: pipeline completes at 108s (should be ~25 minutes for full pipeline)

The graph compiles correctly — compile logs show 'merge_decompose' as a node. But at runtime, the Oracle barrier doesn't collect the 3 generator results.

This worked in the previous neograph version (before the construct_from_functions Construct-acceptance fix in neograph-dqe). The dqe fix may have broken Oracle barrier assembly.

Pipeline: construct_from_functions with @node + sub-construct.map(). The Oracle is on decompose, NOT inside the sub-construct. The sub-construct is verify_claim (explore→score).
notes:
## Reproduction

Minimal repro — pure neograph, no piarch dependencies:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel
from neograph import (
    node, construct_from_functions, compile, run,
    configure_llm, register_scripted, merge_fn,
    ToolInteraction,
)

class Input(BaseModel, frozen=True):
    text: str

class Claims(BaseModel, frozen=True):
    items: list[str]

class Merged(BaseModel, frozen=True):
    final: list[str]

class SubIn(BaseModel, frozen=True):
    claim: str

class SubOut(BaseModel, frozen=True):
    result: str

# Fake LLM
class FakeLLM:
    def with_structured_output(self, model, **kw):
        self._m = model
        return self
    def invoke(self, msgs, **kw):
        return self._m(items=['claim-1', 'claim-2'])

configure_llm(
    llm_factory=lambda tier, **kw: FakeLLM(),
    prompt_compiler=lambda t, d, **kw: [{'role': 'user', 'content': 'test'}],
)

# Oracle node — 3 generators + merge
@merge_fn
def merge_claims(variants: list[Claims]) -> Merged:
    all_items = [i for v in variants for i in v.items]
    return Merged(final=list(set(all_items)))

register_scripted('merge_claims', merge_claims)  # if needed

@node(outputs=Merged, model='fast', prompt='decompose', ensemble_n=3, merge_fn='merge_claims')
def decompose() -> Merged: ...

@node(outputs=list[str])
def flatten(decompose: Merged) -> list[str]:
    print(f'flatten received: {decompose}')  # <-- will show None or empty
    return decompose.final if decompose else []

# Sub-construct (to trigger the dqe code path)
@node(outputs=SubOut, model='fast', prompt='verify')
def explore(item: SubIn) -> SubOut: ...

@node(outputs=SubOut, model='fast', prompt='score')
def score(explore: SubOut) -> SubOut: ...

sub = construct_from_functions('sub', [explore, score], input=SubIn, output=SubOut)

# Parent: @node + sub-construct in one construct_from_functions call
pipeline = construct_from_functions('test', [
    decompose, flatten, sub.map('flatten', key='claim'),
])

graph = compile(pipeline)
result = run(graph, input={'node_id': 'test'})
print(f'Result keys: {sorted(result.keys())}')
print(f'flatten output: {result.get("flatten")}')
```

Expected: merge_claims runs after 3 decompose generators, flatten receives Merged with items.
Actual: flatten receives None/empty, pipeline completes in seconds.

The bug is triggered by having BOTH an Oracle node AND a sub-construct in the same construct_from_functions call. Remove the sub-construct and Oracle works fine.
## Reproduce Attempt
- Wrote 2 regression tests covering both failure modes (Oracle before sub-construct, Oracle after sub-construct)
- BOTH PASS — bug not reproducible with current code (commit 655f8eb, the dqe fix)
- The Oracle barrier assembly is not affected by the sub_by_field dict or topo sort changes
- Possible consumer-side issue: stale install, topology detail not captured, or LLM/prompt setup
- Tests committed as regression guards regardless
## Trace Similar
- Checked all Oracle-related compiler code in compiler.py (_add_node_to_graph Oracle path)
- The dqe changes are purely in decorators.py (_build_construct_from_decorated)
- The compiler receives the final ordered list and processes Node vs Construct independently
- No path from the dqe changes to Oracle barrier wiring — they're in different layers
- The all_known adjacency dict adds sub-construct keys but doesn't modify how Node keys are processed
## Architect Review
- [LOW] Tests cover both orderings (Oracle before/after sub-construct)
- [LOW] Bug not reproducible — likely consumer environment issue
- Recommendation: close as cannot-reproduce, keep regression guards

Overall: ALL_LOW
## Root cause found

The compiled graph only has edges for the first 3 nodes:
```
__start__ → build-catalog → register-artifacts → read-node → __end__
```

ALL other nodes (decompose, merge_decompose, flatten-groups, classify, cluster, etc.) exist in the graph but have ZERO edges connecting them. The Oracle conditional edges were never compiled.

The Mermaid output confirms: only static edges up to read-node, then __end__. The 15 remaining nodes are orphaned.

This is NOT an Oracle-specific bug. It's a compiler edge-wiring bug — the compiler stops adding edges after the first few nodes when construct_from_functions contains a mix of @node items + Construct.map() items.

## Reproduce more specifically

Get the Mermaid from: compile(pipeline).get_graph().draw_mermaid()

Expected: 18 nodes with edges forming the full pipeline topology.
Actual: 3 nodes connected, 15 nodes orphaned, __end__ after read-node.
## Root Cause Analysis (corrected)
- The pipeline DOES work end-to-end at runtime
- Oracle fires: 3 generators → merge → merged result flows to downstream nodes
- The Mermaid rendering (graph.get_graph().draw_mermaid()) omits conditional edges — this is a LangGraph visualization limitation, NOT a neograph edge-wiring bug
- Oracle uses add_conditional_edges which Mermaid renders as dotted lines or omits entirely depending on LangGraph version
- The consumer's logs showing 'flatten-groups starts IMMEDIATELY (no merge event)' needs further investigation on their specific topology (piarch's 18-node pipeline), but our repro shows the pattern works correctly
- Regression guards committed — 2 tests covering both orderings, both pass
close_reason: Not a neograph bug — Oracle+sub-construct works at runtime. Mermaid rendering omits conditional edges (LangGraph visualization issue). 2 regression guards in place.

title: KILLER: Construct.forward() subclass mode for branching logic
description:
Source: DSPy Module.forward, PyTorch nn.Module
Effort: Large (v2 project)
Breaks API: No (new class-based mode)

KILLER IDEA #2. Kills Operator(when='string_key') string dispatch entirely. This is where LangGraph's core pain lives.

Current:
  validate = Node(...) | Operator(when='has_failures')
  # 'has_failures' is a string key into _condition_registry — no type checking

Proposed:
  class Analysis(Construct):
      decompose = Node.produce(output=Claims, ...)
      classify  = Node.produce(output=Classified, ...)
      report    = Node.produce(output=Report, ...)

      def forward(self, topic: str) -> Report:
          claims = self.decompose(topic)
          classified = self.classify(claims)

          # CONTROL FLOW IS JUST PYTHON
          if classified.confidence < 0.7:
              claims = self.decompose(topic, temperature=1.0)
              classified = self.classify(claims)

          if any(c['severity'] == 'critical' for c in classified.issues):
              neograph.interrupt({'reason': 'critical', 'items': classified.issues})

          return self.report(classified)

NeoGraph traces forward() once at compile time (like torch.fx) to build the compiled StateGraph. At runtime, executes like the declarative version.

Wins:
- Conditionals are Python 'if' — no Operator(when='...')
- Loops are Python 'for'
- try/except works
- Type checker sees everything (classified.confidence is a real attribute)
- Testability: instantiate Analysis(), call .forward(topic) directly with fakes

Prior art: torch.fx, jax.make_jaxpr, dspy tracing.

Source: .claude/research/dx_research_040426.md #6

## Resolved design decisions (2026-04-04 spike)

Spike doc: `.claude/spikes/neograph-pub/design.md`
Prototype: `.claude/spikes/neograph-pub/spike.py` (passes — traced forward() produces nodes list equivalent to declarative Construct)

**Tracing strategy: Symbolic Proxy (torch.fx-style).** At compile time, `ForwardConstruct.__init__` discovers `Node` class attributes via `cls.__mro__` walk, builds a `_ForwardSelf` shim whose `__getattr__` returns recording callables, and invokes `forward()` once with a stand-in `Proxy` input. Each node call records an entry; the ordered entries become the `nodes` list passed to `Construct.__init__`, which feeds the existing `compile()` pipeline unchanged.

**Why not AST walking:** brittle on REPL/notebook-defined classes, forces re-implementation of Python name resolution, diverges the IR for control flow. Proxies get name resolution, helper-call pass-through, and straight-line-in-same-IR for free. See design doc for full rationale.

**Why not hybrid:** doubles surface area before proving the simpler version. Proxies can reach conditional support via `__bool__` interception (torch.fx precedent).

**Coexistence with declarative Construct:** `ForwardConstruct` is a subclass of `Construct`. Both styles share one compile pipeline. Users pick per-pipeline.

**MVP scope:** straight-line only. Branches, loops, try/except each get their own subtask with their own design discussion.

## Subtasks

1. neograph-di0 — ForwardConstruct base class + class-attribute node discovery (P2, blocked-by: none)
2. neograph-3us — symbolic-proxy tracer for straight-line forward() (P2, blocked-by: neograph-di0)
3. neograph-vxv — compile() integration for traced ForwardConstruct (P2, blocked-by: neograph-3us)
4. neograph-w5z — Python `if` conditional branching via Proxy.__bool__ (P2, blocked-by: neograph-vxv) — this is the subtask that kills `Operator(when='...')` string dispatch
5. neograph-m8n — Python `for` loop support via Proxy.__iter__ (P3, blocked-by: neograph-w5z)
6. neograph-xi0 — try/except support in forward() tracing (P3, blocked-by: neograph-w5z)

This epic stays open until subtasks 1–4 land (MVP + killer conditional feature). Subtasks 5–6 may defer.
close_reason: ALL 6 SUBTASKS CLOSED. ForwardConstruct epic complete: base class + node discovery (di0), symbolic proxy tracer (3us), compile() integration with zero compiler changes (vxv), Python if branching via Proxy.__bool__ + re-trace (w5z), for-loop via __iter__ → Each (m8n), try/except v1 documented limitation (xi0). Users can write forward() with if/for/try and compile to LangGraph. 195 tests.

title: load_spec(): YAML/JSON → Construct IR loader
description:
## What
The core loader function: takes a YAML/JSON spec (file path or string), validates against the JSON Schema, resolves type/tool/model refs from the project surface, builds Construct IR.

## Approach
1. Parse YAML or JSON (detect by content or extension)
2. Validate against neograph-pipeline.schema.json
3. Load project surface from project.neograph.yaml (or passed config)
4. For each node in spec: resolve outputs type (from type registry), resolve tools (from tool registry), resolve model (from model registry), build Node with appropriate mode/prompt/modifiers
5. For each construct in spec: build sub-Construct with input/output types, apply Loop/Each/Oracle modifiers
6. Build top-level Construct from pipeline.nodes
7. Return compilable Construct

## Integration points
- Type registry (neograph-tmfa)
- Inline prompts (neograph-????)
- Condition evaluator (neograph-????)
- Existing compile() — the loader output is a standard Construct

## Deliverable
- load_spec(path_or_str, project=None) -> Construct in src/neograph/loader.py
- Exported from neograph __init__.py
- Tests: valid spec loads and compiles, missing type ref errors, missing tool ref errors
design:
## Architecture
Single function: load_spec(spec, project=None) -> Construct in src/neograph/loader.py
- spec: str (YAML/JSON string) or dict (pre-parsed)
- project: str (YAML/JSON) or dict or None
- Validates spec against pipeline schema
- Resolves types via lookup_type, tools via lookup (future), models by name
- Builds Node objects with proper mode, scripted_fn, prompt (inline), modifiers
- Builds sub-Constructs from construct specs with Loop/Each/Oracle
- Returns top-level Construct ready for compile()
notes:
## Findings
- spec_types.py: register_type/lookup_type + load_project_types (JSON Schema → Pydantic)
- conditions.py: parse_condition('score < 0.8') → Callable[[Any], bool], regex-based, safe
- _llm.py: _is_inline_prompt detects inline text, _substitute_vars replaces ${key.field}
- Schemas: neograph-pipeline.schema.json + project.neograph.schema.json validated by jsonschema

## Loader algorithm
1. Parse YAML/JSON (detect by content)
2. Validate against pipeline schema
3. Load project surface (if provided), call load_project_types
4. Build nodes dict: for each node spec, lookup_type(outputs), build Node with mode/prompt/modifiers
5. Build constructs: for each construct spec, lookup_type(input/output), build sub-Construct from node refs
6. Apply modifiers: oracle/each/loop/operator on nodes and constructs
7. Build top-level Construct from pipeline.nodes
8. Return compilable Construct

## Core Invariant
load_spec produces a standard Construct identical to what the programmatic API produces — compile() and run() don't know the difference.
close_reason: Implemented in 4ab006d. 6 e2e tests, 518 total passed.

title: @node: mode='produce' default is a silent footgun + dead body for LLM modes
description:
Part of epic neograph-4mx (@node production readiness).

## Problem

Two related footguns that together make @node unusable for LLM modes without a lot of squinting:

### Footgun 1: Default mode is 'produce'

    @node(output=Claims)
    def extract() -> Claims:
        return Claims(items=['...'])

A user reasonably expects this to execute the function body. But `mode='produce'` defaults to an LLM call — and the body is IGNORED. At runtime, the node tries to call the LLM with `prompt=None` and `model=None`, which either:
- Fails with 'LLM not configured' (if configure_llm hasn't run)
- Fails with a cryptic model-dispatch error (if it has)

The user sees a runtime error far from the decoration site. No signal at decoration time that anything is wrong.

### Footgun 2: Function body silently ignored for LLM modes

    @node(mode='produce', output=Claims, prompt='rw/decompose', model='reason')
    def decompose(topic: RawText) -> Claims:
        # This body is DEAD CODE. It never runs.
        return Claims(items=pre_process(topic.text).split('.'))

The user writes real logic in the body. It never executes. The LLM call via `prompt` handles all output generation. The function serves only as a signature carrier (param names → topology, annotations → types).

Nothing — not the docstring, not a warning, not a runtime log — tells the user the body is dead.

## Root cause

`src/neograph/decorators.py:84` defaults `mode='produce'`. Neither `@node` nor `construct_from_module` emits any warning when:
1. `mode='produce'` / 'gather' / 'execute' with `prompt is None` or `model is None` (will fail at runtime)
2. `mode='produce'` / 'gather' / 'execute' with a non-trivial function body (body will be ignored)

## Proposed fix

### Part 1: Infer mode from kwargs when not explicitly set

Change the default from `mode='produce'` to `mode=None` and infer:

    if mode is None:
        if prompt is not None or model is not None:
            mode = 'produce'
        else:
            mode = 'scripted'

This matches user intent: if they pass `prompt=`, they want an LLM call; otherwise they want to run the body.

### Part 2: Fail loud at decoration time on inconsistent configs

After mode inference:

- `mode in ('produce', 'gather', 'execute')` and `prompt is None` → raise `ConstructError` at decoration time: 'LLM modes require prompt=...'
- `mode in ('produce', 'gather', 'execute')` and `model is None` → raise with 'LLM modes require model=...'
- `mode == 'produce'` and function body is non-trivial → emit a `UserWarning` via `warnings.warn` with the message 'The body of @node(mode=\"produce\") functions is not executed; the LLM call via prompt=... provides the output. Move this logic into a scripted node, or remove the body and use \"...\" as placeholder.'

Body triviality check: inspect `fn.__code__.co_code` — a function body of just `...` (Ellipsis) or `pass` or a single `return X` compiles to a small bytecode (<10 bytes). Use that threshold to detect non-trivial bodies.

Alternatively, parse `inspect.getsource(fn)` with `ast` and check if the body is a single `Expr(Constant(...))` or `Pass` or `Return(Constant(...))`. Cleaner than bytecode inspection.

### Part 3: Document the two semantics explicitly

Add to the `@node` docstring a 'Mode semantics' section:

    - mode='scripted' — the function body IS the computation. It runs at node
      execution time with upstream values bound by parameter name.
    - mode='produce' / 'gather' / 'execute' — the function body is IGNORED.
      The function exists only as a signature carrier (param names → topology,
      annotations → input/output types). Execution is handled by the LLM
      dispatch via prompt= and model= kwargs.

## Acceptance criteria

- New test `test_node_decorator_default_mode_infers_scripted_when_no_prompt`: `@node(output=X)` on a function with a body becomes mode='scripted'.
- New test `test_node_decorator_default_mode_infers_produce_when_prompt_present`: `@node(output=X, prompt='...', model='...')` becomes mode='produce'.
- New test `test_node_decorator_produce_without_prompt_raises`: `@node(mode='produce', output=X, model='reason')` (no prompt) raises at decoration time.
- New test `test_node_decorator_gather_without_model_raises`: similar for model=.
- New test `test_node_decorator_produce_with_nontrivial_body_warns`: `@node(mode='produce', prompt='...', model='...', output=X) def f(): real_logic()` emits UserWarning. Catch via `pytest.warns(UserWarning)`.
- New test `test_node_decorator_produce_with_ellipsis_body_no_warn`: `def f(): ...` does NOT warn.
- Existing scripted-mode tests still pass (they all pass `mode='scripted'` explicitly).
- Update `examples/01b_scripted_pipeline_decorator.py` to drop `mode='scripted'` where inference would handle it (reduces boilerplate in the example).

## Files to read

- `src/neograph/decorators.py:81-162` — `node` decorator body
- `src/neograph/factory.py` — look at what happens when mode='produce' runs without prompt (for the runtime error we want to prevent)
- Python stdlib: `ast`, `inspect.getsource`, `warnings.warn`

## Non-goals

- Do NOT change the Node class's `mode` field semantics
- Do NOT remove the `mode=` kwarg — users should still be able to force an explicit mode
- Do NOT delete function bodies in LLM modes (a warning is enough; don't break existing code)
close_reason: Three P1 @node production-readiness fixes landed in parallel team (node-p1-fixes). q54: mode inference (None default → scripted/produce inferred from kwargs) + decoration-time validation (LLM modes require prompt+model) + dead-body AST warning. 6 new tests. 8pt: full fan-in parameter type validation via _validate_fan_in_types in construct_from_module with ForwardRef resolution via get_type_hints+localns. 4 new tests. jyw: Each/fan-out interop via map_over=/map_key= kwargs on @node, sidecar carryover on modifier-copy, fan-out param skipping in adjacency loop, neo_each_item integration in raw_adapter. 6 new tests. Combined: 127/127 passing (+16 new). Examples 01b/02/08 cleaned up to use mode inference.

title: describe_value: BAML-style instance renderer for Pydantic models
description:
Add describe_value() to describe_type.py — renders a Pydantic model INSTANCE in the same TypeScript-style notation as describe_type, but with actual values instead of type names.

## What describe_type produces (schema):
{
  node_id: string  // Graph node identifier
  score: float  // Relevance score 0-1
}

## What describe_value should produce (instance):
{
  node_id: "UC-042"  // Graph node identifier
  score: 0.9  // Relevance score 0-1
}

Same notation. Values where types were. Field descriptions still as comments. One consistent format.

## For lists:
describe_value([hit1, hit2]) produces:
[
  {
    node_id: "UC-042"  // Graph node identifier
    score: 0.9
  },
  {
    node_id: "BR-007"
    score: 0.7
  }
]

## Implementation
Reuse _field_comment from describe_type. Walk model_fields, get value via getattr(instance, field_name). Render value: str→quoted, int/float→bare, bool→true/false, None→null, BaseModel→recursive, list→[items], dict→{k: v}.

## Then update _render_tool_result_for_llm in _llm.py
Replace describe_type schema + renderer.render() with describe_value() only. One format, no XML/BAML mismatch.

## File: src/neograph/describe_type.py
## Also: src/neograph/_llm.py (_render_tool_result_for_llm)
## Also: src/neograph/__init__.py (export describe_value)
## Tests: tests/test_renderers.py (TestDescribeType class — add describe_value tests)

title: context= references never validated against known nodes

Root cause: No validation anywhere checks that context names reference actual upstream nodes.

Fix: In _validate_node_chain, when processing each node, check that every name in node.context matches a known producer field_name. For sub-constructs (construct.input is not None), defer the check since context might come from parent state.

title: Dedupe _type_name helper across factory.py and construct.py
description:
HIGH-02 from code review 040426_2207. Flagged independently by 2 agents (dry + consistency).

Two definitions:
- src/neograph/factory.py:71 — _type_name(t: Any) -> str | None (returns None for None input)
- src/neograph/construct.py:282 — _type_name(tp: Any) -> str (returns literal 'None' for None input)

Same name, divergent return types and None handling. grep -n '^def _type_name' src/neograph/ returns both.

Lowest-friction fix: rename the construct.py version to _fmt_type. The two helpers serve different roles — factory uses None for log-field omission, construct uses 'None' for error rendering. Trying to merge them requires settling the None convention and risking behavioral changes in logging.

Alternative: promote a single canonical helper to a shared module (e.g. src/neograph/_types.py) with one agreed-upon None convention.

Source: .claude/code-review/040426_2207/synthesis.md HIGH-02

title: Fix sub-construct output extraction for Loop nodes
description:
## Problem
make_subgraph_fn scans sub_result.values() with isinstance(val, sub.output) but Loop nodes have list[T] from append-list reducer. Earlier non-loop nodes match first, returning wrong output.

## Fix
In factory.py make_subgraph_fn (~line 700-712):
1. Iterate sub_result values in REVERSE (later pipeline nodes take precedence)
2. Before isinstance check, unwrap lists: if val is list and val[-1] matches output type, use val[-1]

```python
for val in reversed(list(sub_result.values())):
    check_val = val
    if isinstance(val, list) and val:
        check_val = val[-1]
    if isinstance(check_val, sub.output):
        output_val = check_val
        break
```

## File
src/neograph/factory.py (make_subgraph_fn, ~line 700-712)

## Verification
- test_sub_construct_with_internal_loop should get further (may not fully pass until Loop-on-Construct is done)
- Existing tests: uv run pytest -q passes
notes:
## Prep
- Files to modify: src/neograph/factory.py (make_subgraph_fn, lines 700-712)
- Approach: In the output extraction loop, iterate sub_result values in reverse order (later pipeline nodes take precedence). Before isinstance check, unwrap lists — if val is a non-empty list, check val[-1] against sub.output. This handles Loop nodes whose state field is list[T] from the append-list reducer.
## Execution
- Changes made: factory.py make_subgraph_fn — iterate sub_result.values() in reverse, unwrap loop append-lists (val[-1]) before isinstance check
- Quality check: PASS (424 passed, 4 failed — was 5 failed, sub-construct loop test now passes)

title: Each over= examples inconsistent and meaningless in isolation
description:
Different over= paths appear across docs with no shared context:
- clusters.clusters (what-is-neograph)
- clusters.groups (vocabulary, modifiers, subgraphs — 6x)
- items.groups (api.mdx:179)
- discover.groups (each-fanout, api.mdx:241)
- cluster.groups (full-pipeline)
- items (quick-start:195)

Several point at hypothetical state fields that the surrounding snippet never defines. The first part of 'over' is a state field name (a previous node's output), so without a named previous node the example is unrunnable.

Fix: settle on one canonical example (e.g. the each-fanout walkthrough's discover.groups with full context) and reference it by link from vocabulary/modifiers/subgraphs. In docs that don't have a full pipeline, use over=<prev_node>.<field> as a placeholder to signal the pattern. Source: code review issue #7.

title: Each×Oracle fused barrier doesn't propagate config to merge_fn DI params
description: In _merge_one_group (compiler.py:494), config is passed but _resolve_merge_args receives it. However, the config at barrier time may not have configurable populated correctly since the barrier runs in the outer graph context, not inside a Send. FromInput/FromConfig in @merge_fn within fused Each×Oracle topology resolves to None. Workaround: use state params (from_state) instead of DI in merge_fn when using Each×Oracle fusion.
close_reason: Invalid: DI in fused barrier works correctly. Test test_each_oracle_with_decorated_merge_fn proves FromInput resolves in the fused topology.

title: Conditional produce: skip_when= predicate to bypass LLM call
design:
## Architecture (from analysis session 2026-04-06)

### New Node fields (node.py)
```python
skip_when: Callable[[Any], bool] | None = None
skip_value: Callable[[Any], Any] | None = None
```
Same pattern as raw_fn, renderer, fan_out_param. Justified: programmatic API needs it too.

### Factory integration (factory.py)
Insert after _extract_input, BEFORE renderer and invoke_structured:
```python
input_data = _extract_input(state, node)
# skip_when check
if node.skip_when is not None and node.skip_when(input_data):
    skip_result = node.skip_value(input_data) if node.skip_value else None
    node_log.info('node_skipped', reason='skip_when')
    if skip_result is not None:
        return {field_name: skip_result}
    return {}
# renderer + invoke_structured follow...
```
Apply in _make_produce_fn AND _make_tool_fn (gather/execute). NOT in _make_scripted_wrapper (scripted nodes have Python control flow).

### @node decorator (decorators.py)
Add skip_when= and skip_value= kwargs, pass through to Node().

### Each interaction
skip_when receives one item at a time (via _extract_input's neo_each_item path). Per-item skip decision. Missing items = fewer keys in the dict reducer. Document this.

### Renderer interaction
skip evaluates BEFORE rendering. No point rendering if we skip.

### Tests (~8)
- test_produce_skip_when_true: skip fires, no LLM call, skip_value returned
- test_produce_skip_when_false: normal LLM call
- test_skip_when_no_skip_value: returns empty dict (pass-through skip)
- test_skip_when_with_each_fanout: per-item skip
- test_skip_when_gather_mode: skip before tool loop
- test_skip_when_programmatic_api: Node(..., skip_when=lambda x: True)
- test_skip_when_decorator_surface: @node(skip_when=...)
- test_skip_when_three_surfaces: all three API surfaces

### Files to modify
- src/neograph/node.py (2 fields)
- src/neograph/factory.py (_make_produce_fn + _make_tool_fn)
- src/neograph/decorators.py (kwarg passthrough)
- tests/test_e2e_piarch_ready.py (new TestConditionalProduce class)

### Core invariant
skip_when is evaluated after _extract_input but before renderer and LLM call. When it returns truthy, zero LLM tokens consumed. Existing nodes without skip_when are completely unaffected.
notes:
## Research (verified against current code)

### Insertion points confirmed
- _make_produce_fn: line 190 (_extract_input) → INSERT skip_when → line 192 (renderer)
- _make_tool_fn: line 238 (_extract_input) → INSERT skip_when → line 240 (renderer)

### Node fields location
- node.py: after fan_out_param (line ~68), before model_config. Two new Callable | None fields.

### @node decorator
- decorators.py node() signature: add skip_when and skip_value kwargs. Pass through to Node(). Zero sidecar needed.

### Core Invariant
skip_when is evaluated after _extract_input but before renderer and LLM call. When truthy, zero LLM tokens. Existing nodes unaffected (skip_when defaults to None).
## Architect Review
- [LOW] skip_when/skip_value on Node: same justification as renderer/fan_out_param. Programmatic API needs it.
- [LOW] Each + skip returning empty dict: fewer keys in barrier dict. Documented, not a bug.
- [LOW] No skip for scripted nodes: correct — scripted nodes have Python control flow.
Overall: ALL_LOW
## Triage: Clean review — proceeding to implement.

title: Restrict Each+Loop on same node (ConstructError)
description:
## Problem
Compiler has exclusive modifier dispatch: Each takes priority, Loop silently dropped. Each+Loop composition on a single node is unsupported by design.

## Fix
Two locations:

### 1. _construct_validation.py — assembly-time check
In _validate_node_chain, add after existing Loop reenter check:
```python
if isinstance(item, Node):
    if item.has_modifier(Each) and item.has_modifier(Loop):
        raise ConstructError(
            f"Node '{item.name}' has both Each and Loop modifiers. "
            f"These cannot be combined on a single node. "
            f"Use a sub-construct with Loop inside an Each fan-out instead."
        )
```

### 2. modifiers.py — immediate feedback at | time
In Modifiable.__or__, check before applying:
```python
if isinstance(modifier, Loop) and self.has_modifier(Each):
    raise ConstructError(...)
if isinstance(modifier, Each) and self.has_modifier(Loop):
    raise ConstructError(...)
```

## Files
- src/neograph/_construct_validation.py
- src/neograph/modifiers.py

## Verification
- New test: node | Each(...) | Loop(...) raises ConstructError
- New test: node | Loop(...) | Each(...) raises ConstructError
- Existing tests pass
notes:
## Prep
- Files to modify: src/neograph/_construct_validation.py (_validate_node_chain), src/neograph/modifiers.py (Modifiable.__or__)
- Approach: Add Each+Loop mutual exclusion in two places: (1) Modifiable.__or__ for immediate feedback at pipe-time, (2) _validate_node_chain for catch-all at construct assembly. Both raise ConstructError with message suggesting sub-construct pattern.
## Execution
- Changes: modifiers.py Modifiable.__or__ — Each+Loop mutual exclusion check. _construct_validation.py — belt-and-suspenders assembly-time check. decorators.py — early check for map_over + loop_when (decorator applies Each then returns, bypassing __or__)
- Quality: 424 passed, 4 failed (same 4 known loop failures)

title: .map() method replacing Each(over=..., key=...) string path
description:
Source: Prefect .map(), Airflow .expand()
Effort: Medium
Breaks API: No (additive, keeps Each internally)

Problem: Each(over='cluster.groups', key='label') is a string-pathed access to state. Fragile, invisible to type checkers, no IDE autocomplete, breaks on refactor.

Proposed:
  # Option A: lambda (mypy-friendly)
  verify = Node('verify', input=ClusterGroup, output=VerifyResult).map(
      lambda s: s.cluster.groups, key='label'
  )

  # Option B: with param inference (best)
  verify = Node('verify', input=ClusterGroup, output=VerifyResult).map(clusters)
  # 'clusters' is a variable bound to an upstream node handle

Internals: .map() is sugar over Each — it still builds the same modifier. Implementation is a method on Node that returns self | Each(over=..., key=...). The lambda is introspected or executed once at compile time to resolve the path.

Source: .claude/research/dx_research_040426.md #2
close_reason: Implemented .map() as sugar over Each on Modifiable. Supports lambda introspection via _PathRecorder and string passthrough. 9 new tests covering lambda resolution, string form, semantic equivalence to | Each, end-to-end fan-out, error cases (empty path, scalar return, indexing, wrong type), and Construct inheritance. 85/85 passing.

title: CRITICAL: FromInput shadows upstream node — silent dependency drop
description:
When a @node param name matches both an upstream node AND has Annotated[T, FromInput], the DI classifier wins and the dependency edge is silently dropped. The pipeline executes in wrong order or skips type validation entirely.

Root cause: decorators.py line 639 — `if p.name in param_res: continue` unconditionally skips DI-classified params from the inputs dict. The adjacency builder at line 1249 does the same.

Fix: In _build_construct_from_decorated, after building the decorated dict (line 1149) and before adjacency wiring (line 1232), check if any DI param name collides with a node in the decorated dict or sub_by_field. Raise ConstructError on collision.

Fixture: tests/check_fixtures/known_gaps/di_from_input_shadows_upstream.py — move to should_fail/ when fixed.
close_reason: Fixed: DI param name collision with upstream node now raises ConstructError

title: @node: mode='raw' escape hatch (replaces @raw_node for LangGraph power users)
description:
Part of epic neograph-4mx (@node production readiness). Surfaced by the Path A Dogfood epic.

## Problem

`@raw_node` (`src/neograph/node.py:151-175`) provides the LangGraph escape hatch — write a full `(state, config) -> state_update` function and let NeoGraph wire edges + observability around it. It's used for cases where the standard `mode='scripted'` / `mode='produce'` abstractions aren't enough: custom state manipulation, cross-node state reads, conditional updates driven by full state inspection.

Path A positioning says @node is THE public API. Having a separate `@raw_node` decorator splits the mental model: users learn @node for 95% of cases and have to context-switch to @raw_node for the escape hatch. Dogfooding examples will hit this when `examples/06_raw_node_escape_hatch.py` gets migrated.

## Proposed API

Fold @raw_node into @node via a new mode:

    @node(mode='raw', input=Claims, output=Claims)
    def filter_claims(state, config):
        \"\"\"Custom filtering logic with full state + config access.\"\"\"
        claims = None
        for field_name in state.__class__.model_fields:
            val = getattr(state, field_name, None)
            if isinstance(val, Claims):
                claims = val
                break
        if claims is None:
            return {'filter_claims': Claims(items=[])}
        filtered = Claims(items=[c for c in claims.items if c != 'b'])
        return {'filter_claims': filtered}

Signature constraint: `mode='raw'` functions must have exactly two parameters `(state, config)` — the LangGraph convention. Same shape as @raw_node today. They are NOT wired through parameter-name topology (the function body handles its own state access). The `input=` / `output=` kwargs still declare the node's typed contract for the validator and state builder.

`construct_from_module` discovers raw-mode @nodes via the sidecar but SKIPS the parameter-name adjacency pass for them — their parameters are fixed (state, config), not upstream names.

## Implementation sketch

In `src/neograph/decorators.py`:

    def node(
        fn=None,
        *,
        mode='produce',
        ...,
    ):
        def decorator(f):
            sig = inspect.signature(f)

            if mode == 'raw':
                # Raw mode: enforce (state, config) signature
                params = list(sig.parameters.values())
                if len(params) != 2:
                    raise ConstructError(
                        f\"@node(mode='raw') '{f.__name__}' must have exactly two \"
                        f\"parameters (state, config); got {len(params)}.\"
                    )
                if [p.name for p in params] != ['state', 'config']:
                    raise ConstructError(
                        f\"@node(mode='raw') '{f.__name__}' parameters must be \"
                        f\"named 'state' and 'config'; got {[p.name for p in params]}.\"
                    )
                # Param_names for raw mode: empty tuple — not used for topology.
                param_names = ()
            else:
                # Existing logic: reject *args/**kwargs, collect param names
                ...

            node_name = (name or f.__name__).replace('_', '-')
            # ... (existing output/input inference)

            n = Node(
                name=node_name,
                mode='scripted' if mode == 'raw' else mode,  # raw dispatches via raw_fn like @raw_node
                input=inferred_input,
                output=inferred_output,
                ...,
                raw_fn=f if mode == 'raw' else None,
            )

            _register_sidecar(n, f, param_names)
            # Stash the original mode so construct_from_module knows to skip
            # adjacency for raw nodes. Augment sidecar tuple with mode.
            return n

In `construct_from_module`, when building adjacency:
- For raw-mode @nodes (detectable via sidecar mode or `n.raw_fn is not None`), skip the parameter-name resolution loop. They have no upstream deps inferred from signature; they depend on whatever they choose to read from state.
- Raw-mode nodes are still included in the topological sort at their declaration point (since they have no incoming edges, they'll float to the start unless something else depends on them; use declaration-order tiebreaking).

## Acceptance criteria

- New tests in `TestNodeDecorator`:
  - `test_node_decorator_raw_mode_basic`: a `@node(mode='raw')` function reads a Claims value from state and returns a filtered update dict. End-to-end via compile+run.
  - `test_node_decorator_raw_mode_wrong_signature_raises`: three params, or wrong names ('s', 'c'), raises ConstructError at decoration time.
  - `test_node_decorator_raw_mode_with_downstream`: a raw @node produces output that a downstream scripted @node consumes by parameter name. Verifies the output=X kwarg is honored for topology.
  - `test_node_decorator_mixed_raw_and_scripted`: a pipeline with both raw and scripted @nodes in the same module.
- Existing `TestRawNode::test_raw_node_in_pipeline` (which uses the @raw_node decorator) still passes — @raw_node remains importable for back-compat within the codebase, but the Dogfood epic migrates the example to @node(mode='raw').
- @node docstring updated with a raw-mode section.
- Full suite stays green.

## Decision: deprecate @raw_node?

Option 1: keep @raw_node as an alias that calls @node(mode='raw') internally. No user-facing deprecation. Reader finds both in docs → confused.

Option 2: delete @raw_node from public API. Since no external users, this is safe. All internal usages migrate.

RECOMMENDED: Option 2 — delete @raw_node. This is the whole point of Path A. Documentation simplification wins.

Execute deletion as part of the Dogfood epic (migrate `examples/06_raw_node_escape_hatch.py` to @node(mode='raw') and drop @raw_node from `neograph/__init__.py`). This subtask only ADDS the @node(mode='raw') support; the deletion happens later so as not to break intermediate states.

## Dependencies

- No hard deps — can start immediately. The sidecar carryover (neograph-jyw) isn't needed because raw-mode nodes don't compose with modifiers in v1.

## Files to read

- `src/neograph/node.py:151-175` — @raw_node current implementation
- `src/neograph/decorators.py` — @node
- `src/neograph/factory.py::_make_raw_wrapper` — how raw_fn is dispatched at runtime (already handles the state-update-dict return shape)
- `tests/test_e2e_piarch_ready.py::TestRawNode::test_raw_node_in_pipeline` — reference test

## Non-goals

- Do NOT delete @raw_node in this subtask (deletion is part of Dogfood epic).
- Do NOT add support for raw mode combined with Each / Oracle / Operator modifiers — raw mode is the escape hatch, users manipulating state manually don't need framework modifiers.

title: Each×Oracle fusion: flat M×N Send topology for map_over + ensemble_n
description:
## Problem

When a node needs both fan-out (Each/map_over) and ensemble (Oracle/ensemble_n), the current workaround is a sub-construct wrapping the Oracle, then Each over the sub-construct. This produces a nested topology: outer graph with Each + inner sub-graph with Oracle. The nested approach adds sub-graph compilation overhead, two state models, and extra nodes.

A flat M×N Send() topology is simpler, fewer nodes, and what a LangGraph expert would write by hand.

## Use Case: Chunked Decompose

Large documents (300+ lines) exceed R1's thinking budget. Solution: chunk by sections, decompose each chunk independently with Oracle(n=3).

\`\`\`python
@node(outputs=ClaimGroupingResult, model="reason", prompt="rw/decompose",
      ensemble_n=3, merge_fn="group_claims",
      map_over="chunk_document", map_key="chunk_idx")
def decompose(chunk: ReadContext) -> ClaimGroupingResult: ...
\`\`\`

For 2 chunks × 3 Oracle variants = 6 LLM calls, all concurrent.

## Design: Flat M×N Topology

### Topology

\`\`\`
chunk_document → flat_router → Send(gen, {chunk=A, gen=0, model=reason})
                              ├→ Send(gen, {chunk=A, gen=1, model=fast})
                              ├→ Send(gen, {chunk=A, gen=2, model=creative})
                              ├→ Send(gen, {chunk=B, gen=0, model=reason})
                              ├→ Send(gen, {chunk=B, gen=1, model=fast})
                              └→ Send(gen, {chunk=B, gen=2, model=creative})

gen(*)  →  group_merge_barrier  →  merge_chunks  →  END
           [defer=True]
           [groups by each_key, calls merge_fn per group]
\`\`\`

4 nodes, 1 graph, 0 sub-graphs. All M×N generators run concurrently.

### Changes Required

**1. Remove Oracle+Each mutual exclusion** (modifiers.py + _construct_validation.py)
The guard we added in neograph-bm6e needs to be removed (or relaxed to allow composition).

**2. Compiler: _wire_each_oracle fusion** (compiler.py)
New function that handles the fused pattern:
- flat_router: iterates collection × oracle.n, sends M×N with (neo_each_item, neo_oracle_gen_id, neo_oracle_model)
- gen_fn: same as regular Oracle generator, reads neo_each_item for input
- group_merge_barrier: groups collected results by each.key, calls merge_fn per group
- Output: dict[str, MergedResult] (same as regular Each output)

**3. State model: fused collector field** (state.py)
- Collector field stores tagged results: list[tuple[str, T]] where str is the each key
- Output field is dict[str, T] (same as regular Each)
- Counter field for the group_merge_barrier

**4. Factory: gen_fn reads neo_each_item** (factory.py)
The generator function must extract input from neo_each_item (Each pattern) instead of from a named upstream field. This is the Node.fan_out_param mechanism already exists for Each nodes.

**5. Decorator: Each+Oracle composition** (decorators.py)
When @node sees both map_over and ensemble_n:
- Build Node with BOTH Each and Oracle modifiers (order: Each first, Oracle second)
- The compiler detects the combination and routes to _wire_each_oracle

**6. Validation** (_construct_validation.py)
- effective_producer_type for Each+Oracle: dict[str, output] (same as Each alone — Oracle merge produces the per-item result)
- Downstream consumers see dict[str, ClaimGroupingResult], can consume as list[ClaimGroupingResult]

### merge_prompt handling
For LLM-based merge (merge_prompt instead of merge_fn), the group_merge_barrier calls invoke_structured M times. These can be batched with asyncio.gather for parallelism inside the single barrier node. No sub-graph needed even for expensive merges.

### What stays the same
- @node DX (just add both kwargs)
- Downstream consumers see dict[str, T] (same as Each alone)
- merge_fn receives list[T] (same as Oracle alone)
- Type validation, error messages, all modifiers except the mutual exclusion
- list[T] consumer of dict[str, T] unwrap (merge-after-fanout)

### Acceptance Criteria
1. @node with map_over + ensemble_n compiles and runs correctly
2. M×N generators all run concurrently (flat Send topology)
3. merge_fn called once per item with that item's N variants
4. Output is dict[str, MergedResult] (standard Each shape)
5. Downstream list[T] consumer works (dict→list unwrap)
6. Tests: e2e with scripted merge_fn, e2e with merge_prompt (if LLM configured)
7. Fixture: should_pass for valid Each+Oracle, should_fail for type mismatches
design:
## Architecture Decision: Flat M×N vs Nested Sub-graph

### Flat (CHOSEN)
- Single router dispatches M×N Send() calls
- Single barrier groups by each_key, calls merge_fn per group
- 4 nodes, 1 graph, 0 sub-graphs
- All M×N generators concurrent

### Nested (REJECTED)
- Each router dispatches M sub-graph invocations
- Each sub-graph has Oracle router + N generators + merge barrier
- 4 outer + 2 inner nodes, 2 graphs
- More overhead, no parallelism advantage for scripted merge_fn

### Tradeoff
Flat wins for scripted merge_fn (microsecond merge, no sub-graph overhead).
For merge_prompt (LLM merge), batch with asyncio.gather inside the barrier.

## Core Invariant
Each×Oracle fusion must produce the same result as the nested sub-construct
workaround: for each item in the collection, the merge_fn receives exactly N
Oracle variants for THAT item, not a mix of variants from different items.

## Implementation Order
1. State model (new fused collector type)
2. Compiler (_wire_each_oracle)
3. Factory (gen_fn with Each input + Oracle model routing)
4. Remove mutual exclusion guard (modifiers.py + validation.py)
5. Decorator (map_over + ensemble_n composition)
6. Validation (effective_producer_type, downstream compat)
7. Tests + fixtures
notes:
## Grouping Mechanism

The each.key (already required by Each) IS the grouping tag. No new tagging needed.

1. flat_router sends M×N, each Send carries neo_each_item (which has the key field)
2. gen_fn extracts key from neo_each_item, writes (key, result) to collector
3. Collector reducer appends tagged tuples: list[tuple[str, T]]
4. group_merge_barrier: defaultdict(list) groups by key → M groups of N variants
5. merge_fn called per group → dict[str, MergedResult] (standard Each shape)

The downstream node sees N results (one per item), NOT M×N. Oracle is invisible.

## State Model Detail

\`\`\`python
fields = {
    # Tagged collector: (each_key, generator_result) tuples
    f"neo_eachoracle_{field_name}": (
        Annotated[list[tuple[str, output_type]], _append_tagged],
        [],
    ),
    # Final output: standard Each dict shape
    field_name: (
        Annotated[dict[str, output_type], _merge_dicts],
        {},
    ),
}
\`\`\`

The collector is an internal field (neo_ prefix, stripped from results).
The output field is the standard Each dict — consumers don't know Oracle was involved.
close_reason: Shipped: flat M×N Send topology. 3 e2e tests (programmatic, decorator, list consumer). 768 tests pass.

title: construct_from_functions: Construct.__init__ validator rejects Each fan-out item params
description: After the _build_construct_from_decorated refactor, Construct.__init__ runs _validate_node_chain which checks that every Node.inputs key matches an upstream producer. But Each fan-out item params (e.g. 'group: IndexedClaimGroup' in a node with map_over) don't match any upstream — they're the Each item, not a dependency. _build_construct_from_decorated correctly detects fan_out_params but _validate_node_chain doesn't know about them. Error: 'Node canonicalize declares inputs[group]=IndexedClaimGroup but no upstream node named group exists.' The decorator-level fan-in validator (line 859) correctly skips fan-out params. The Construct-level validator (line 93) needs the same skip.
close_reason: Fixed: validator now infers fan-out receiver from Each modifier when fan_out_param is unset (programmatic API). Three-surface parity testing rule added to AGENTS.md.

title: Factory: extract context fields from state, pass to prompt compiler
